title: Variables & Immutability
description: Elixir variables are immutable bindings. The = operator is a pattern match, not assignment. Variables can be rebound (shadowed) but the original value never changes.

concept.definition: In Elixir, = performs pattern matching. Binding a name to a value creates an immutable reference. Rebinding a name (x = 2 after x = 1) creates a new binding — it does not mutate the original. Use the pin operator ^ to match against the current value of a variable.
concept.analogy: Think of Elixir variables as sticky notes on boxes. You can move the sticky note to a new box (rebind), but you cannot change what is inside the original box (immutable value).
keyPoints: = is pattern matching, not assignment
Bindings are immutable — values never change in place
Rebinding creates a new binding (the name points to a new value)
^ (pin operator) matches against the current binding without rebinding

defaultCode:
x = 42
y = 3.14
name = "Elixir"
IO.puts(x)
IO.puts(y)
IO.puts(name)

title: Atoms & Booleans
description: Atoms are named constants whose value is their own name. Booleans (true/false) and nil are atoms. :ok and :error are the idiomatic success/failure atoms.

concept.definition: An atom is a constant whose name IS its value — :ok, :error, :hello. Atoms are extremely efficient (stored as integers internally). true, false, and nil are aliases for the atoms :true, :false, :nil. Atoms are used everywhere: function return tags, map keys, module names.
concept.analogy: Atoms are like enum constants — :ok is always exactly :ok, just as Color.RED is always RED. Two atoms with the same name are always the same atom.
keyPoints: :atom_name — prefixed with colon
true, false, nil are atoms (aliases for :true, :false, :nil)
Atoms are constants — their value is their name
Used as tags in tuples: {:ok, value} and {:error, reason}

title: Strings
description: Elixir strings are UTF-8 binary sequences. Double-quoted only. Interpolation with #{expr}. The String module provides length, upcase, split, contains?, and more.

concept.definition: Elixir strings are UTF-8 encoded binaries, written with double quotes. String interpolation uses #{expr} to embed any expression. The String module (String.length, String.upcase, String.split, String.contains?) provides utilities. Note: String.length counts Unicode graphemes — byte_size counts raw bytes.
concept.analogy: Elixir strings are like sealed UTF-8 envelopes. You cannot change a letter inside — but you can create a new envelope that combines or transforms the content.
keyPoints: Always double-quoted — single quotes are charlists, a different type
Interpolation: #{expr} embeds any expression
Immutable — String functions return new strings
String.length counts characters; byte_size counts bytes

title: Functions
description: Named functions use def inside defmodule. Anonymous functions use fn -> end. The & capture shorthand creates compact function references.

concept.definition: Named functions (def) live inside modules (defmodule). Private functions use defp. Anonymous functions are created with fn args -> body end and called with func.(args). The & capture operator creates shorthand anonymous functions: &(&1 * 2) is fn x -> x * 2 end. Functions are first-class — pass them, return them, store them.
concept.analogy: Named functions (def) are like published recipes in a cookbook (module). Anonymous functions (fn) are like ad-hoc instructions written on a napkin — useful right now, passed around, but not in the cookbook.
keyPoints: def name(args) do ... end — named function inside a module
fn args -> body end — anonymous function
Call anonymous fn with dot: func.(arg)
& shorthand: &(&1 + &2) is fn a, b -> a + b end

defaultCode:
double = fn x -> x * 2 end
IO.puts(double.(5))
add = &(&1 + &2)
IO.puts(add.(3, 4))

title: Control Flow: if, cond, case
description: if/unless for simple binary checks. cond for multi-branch conditions. case for pattern-matching against a value. All are expressions returning a value.

concept.definition: Elixir control flow constructs are all expressions. if cond do ... else ... end handles binary branching. unless is the negated if. cond do clause -> result end matches the first true clause. case value do pattern -> result end matches the first pattern.
concept.analogy: if is a coin flip (two outcomes). cond is a decision tree (first true branch wins). case is a type sorter (first matching shape wins).
keyPoints: if/unless for simple true/false branching
cond for multi-branch conditions (like chained if-elseif)
case for pattern matching against a specific value
All three are expressions — they return the matched branch's value

defaultCode:
x = 7
result = if x > 0 do
  "positive"
else
  "non-positive"
end
IO.puts(result)

title: Pattern Matching
description: The = operator pattern-matches. Destructure tuples, lists, and maps. Use _ to ignore values. The pin operator ^ matches without rebinding.

concept.definition: In Elixir, = is the match operator. {:ok, value} = {:ok, 42} binds value=42. [head | tail] = [1,2,3] binds head=1, tail=[2,3]. _ is the anonymous variable that ignores its match. case and function clauses use pattern matching implicitly. ^ (pin) prevents rebinding: ^x = 5 fails if x != 5.
concept.analogy: Pattern matching is like a jigsaw puzzle check — you press a shape against the data. If they fit, named holes (variables) are filled with the matching pieces. If they don't fit, a MatchError is raised.
keyPoints: = matches shapes — variables are filled by matching positions
{:ok, val} = result — destructure a tuple
[h | t] = list — head/tail destructure
_ ignores a position. ^var pins to current value.

title: Lists & Enum
description: Elixir lists are linked lists. Prepend with [h|t]. The Enum module provides map, filter, reduce, each, and more for any enumerable.

concept.definition: Elixir lists are singly-linked lists. Prepending is O(1): [0 | list]. Random access is O(n). The Enum module works on any enumerable (list, range, map). Enum.map transforms, Enum.filter selects, Enum.reduce accumulates. length() for list length.
concept.analogy: A list is like a chain of paper clips. Adding to the front is instant (clip on). Getting to the 100th clip requires traversing the chain.
keyPoints: [1, 2, 3] — list literal
[h | t] prepend/destructure
Enum.map(list, fn x -> ... end) — transform each element
Enum.filter and Enum.reduce for selection and accumulation

defaultCode:
nums = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
doubled = Enum.map(nums, fn x -> x * 2 end)
IO.inspect(doubled)
evens = Enum.filter(nums, fn x -> rem(x, 2) == 0 end)
IO.inspect(evens)

title: Tuples
description: Tuples are fixed-size ordered collections stored contiguously. {:ok, value} and {:error, reason} are the idiomatic success/error tags.

concept.definition: Elixir tuples store elements contiguously in memory (unlike lists). Access by index with elem(t, i) (0-indexed). tuple_size(t) returns the count. Tuples are immutable — use put_elem(t, i, val) to create a modified copy. The {:ok, value} / {:error, reason} pattern is ubiquitous in Elixir APIs.
concept.analogy: A tuple is like a fixed-size tray of labeled compartments. You always know exactly how many slots there are and can reach any slot instantly — but you cannot add or remove slots.
keyPoints: {a, b, c} — tuple literal
elem(t, i) — 0-indexed access
tuple_size(t) — number of elements
{:ok, val} / {:error, reason} — idiomatic result tag pattern

defaultCode:
result = {:ok, 42}
{:ok, value} = result
IO.puts(value)
IO.puts(elem(result, 0))
IO.puts(tuple_size(result))

title: Maps
description: Maps are key-value stores. %{key: value} for atom keys. %{"key" => value} for string keys. Map.get, Map.put, Map.update for operations. | syntax for field update.

concept.definition: Elixir maps (%{}) are the go-to key-value structure. Atom keys allow dot-like access in structs: map.key. Map.get(m, key, default) for safe access. Map.put(m, key, val) returns a NEW map (immutable). The update syntax %{map | key: new_val} creates a new map with one field changed. Maps are unordered.
concept.analogy: Maps are like spreadsheets — each row is a key-value pair. Changing a cell produces a new spreadsheet (immutable), not an in-place edit.
keyPoints: %{key: value} for atom keys, %{"key" => value} for string keys
map.key or map[:key] for atom key access
Map.get(m, key, default) for safe lookup
%{map | key: new_val} to create an updated map

title: Modules & Named Functions
description: defmodule groups named functions. def for public, defp for private. Module attributes (@name) for constants. Functions match on clauses in definition order.

concept.definition: defmodule Name do ... end defines a module. def name(args) do ... end declares a public function. defp is private. Multiple clauses of the same function are matched top-to-bottom — this replaces if/case inside functions. Module attributes @attr value define compile-time constants.
concept.analogy: A module is like a toolbox. Named functions are tools inside. defp tools are for internal use only. Multiple clauses are like the same tool that works differently based on what you hand it.
keyPoints: defmodule Name do ... end wraps related functions
def for public, defp for private
Multiple clauses with pattern matching replace if/case
@attribute for module-level constants

defaultCode:
defmodule Greeter do
  def greet("Alice"), do: "Hi Alice!"
  def greet(name), do: "Hello, #{name}!"
end
IO.puts(Greeter.greet("Alice"))
IO.puts(Greeter.greet("Bob"))

title: Recursion
description: Elixir has no mutable loops — recursion with pattern-matched base cases replaces for/while. Tail recursion with accumulators avoids stack overflow.

concept.definition: Since Elixir values are immutable, traditional mutation-based loops (for i = 0; i < n; i++) do not exist. Recursion with a base case replaces iteration. Tail-recursive functions (where the recursive call is the last expression) are optimized by Elixir's runtime to run in constant stack space — use an accumulator argument pattern for this.
concept.analogy: Recursion is like a set of Russian nesting dolls. Each doll (function call) opens to find a smaller doll (recursive call), until you reach the smallest doll (base case) and stop.
keyPoints: Base case (empty list, 0) handled by a pattern-matched clause
Recursive case reduces the problem (smaller list, n-1)
Tail recursion: recursive call is the last operation
Accumulator pattern: carry the result as an argument

defaultCode:
defmodule Math do
  def sum([]), do: 0
  def sum([h | t]), do: h + sum(t)
end
IO.puts(Math.sum([1, 2, 3, 4, 5]))

title: Pipe Operator |>
description: The |> operator passes the result of the left expression as the first argument to the right function. Transforms nested calls into a readable left-to-right pipeline.

concept.definition: The pipe operator |> takes the value on the left and passes it as the FIRST argument to the function on the right. x |> f(y) is equivalent to f(x, y). This eliminates deeply nested function calls and reads naturally left-to-right as a data transformation pipeline.
concept.analogy: The pipe operator is like an assembly line conveyor belt. Each station (function) receives the item from the previous station, transforms it, and passes it on to the next.
keyPoints: val |> f(args) is f(val, args) — val inserted as first argument
Eliminates nested calls: f(g(h(x))) becomes x |> h() |> g() |> f()
Reads left-to-right as a data transformation pipeline
Each step in the pipe should take the piped value as its first argument

defaultCode:
result = "  Hello, Elixir!  "
  |> String.trim()
  |> String.downcase()
  |> String.replace("elixir", "world")
IO.puts(result)
